items.q: What does it mean that RAM is volatile?
items.correct: Its contents are lost when power is removed.
items.misconception: It can explode or be damaged easily.
items.why: Students take 'volatile' from chemistry literally. In computing, volatile means contents don't persist without power - that's why RAM is used for working memory, not storage.

items.q: What is the scope of a local variable?
items.correct: Accessible only within the function/block where it is declared.
items.random_wrong: Stored on a local computer rather than a server.
items.why: Students confuse 'local' with 'on this computer'. In programming, scope refers to the CODE REGION where the variable name resolves - local variables vanish when the function returns.

items.q: What happens when 11111111 + 1 is computed in an 8-bit register?
items.correct: Overflow - the result requires 9 bits but only 8 are available.
items.why: Students miss that registers have fixed width. The mathematical result is 9 bits, but the 8-bit register drops the carry-out, producing 00000000 with an overflow flag.

items.q: Which form requires atomic values (one value per cell)?
items.why: It's 1NF. “2NF” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What is a benefit of normalisation?
items.why: It's Easier consistent updates. “More duplicated data” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many normal forms are usually covered at GCSE (1NF through 3NF)?
items.why: The real figure is 3. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
